learning_objectives: Distinguish between gamification, game-based learning, and serious games
Summarize the research evidence on games for learning
Identify the motivational and cognitive mechanisms by which games support learning
readings: Plass, J. et al. - Handbook of Game-Based Learning Ch. 1-2
Clark, D. et al. - Digital Games, Design, and Learning: A Systematic Review (meta-analysis)

lecture_notes.introduction: Games have unique properties — agency, challenge, feedback loops, narrative — that can powerfully support learning when harnessed intentionally. But slapping points and badges on a worksheet is not game-based learning.
key_concepts: Gamification: adding game elements (points, badges, leaderboards) to non-game contexts
Game-based learning: using games as the primary instructional method
Serious games: games designed specifically for learning, training, or social change
Flow theory: the optimal balance of challenge and skill (Csikszentmihalyi)
Meta-analytic findings: moderate positive effect sizes for well-designed educational games
lecture_notes.demonstration: Play and analyze three educational games (Foldit, DragonBox, iCivics): identify the learning mechanics, motivational elements, and alignment between game mechanics and learning objectives
setup_requirements: Access to Foldit, DragonBox, and iCivics (browser or app)

review.value: Gamification adds extrinsic game elements to existing tasks; GBL integrates learning objectives into the core game mechanics so that playing the game IS learning
Csikszentmihalyi's flow state occurs when challenge matches skill level; games naturally adjust difficulty to maintain flow, which is why they are so engaging

class_schedule.topic: Learning Mechanics and Game Mechanics Alignment
learning_objectives: Map learning mechanics to game mechanics using the LM-GM framework
Design game mechanics that produce specific types of learning
Evaluate existing educational games for LM-GM alignment
readings: Arnab, S. et al. - Mapping Learning and Game Mechanics for Serious Games Analysis
Gee, J.P. - What Video Games Have to Teach Us About Learning and Literacy Ch. 2-3

lecture_notes.introduction: The critical design challenge is ensuring that game mechanics produce learning, not just engagement. If a student can win the game without understanding the content, the game has failed as instruction.
key_concepts: Learning mechanics: actions that produce learning (observation, experimentation, reflection, feedback)
Game mechanics: rules and systems that create gameplay (resource management, pattern recognition, spatial reasoning)
LM-GM mapping: ensuring game mechanics require the cognitive processes that produce learning
Intrinsic integration: learning is inseparable from gameplay
Endogenous vs. exogenous game elements
lecture_notes.demonstration: LM-GM mapping exercise: analyze Minecraft Education Edition and map three game mechanics to the learning mechanics they activate
setup_requirements: Access to Minecraft Education Edition or video walkthrough

review.value: The gold standard: learning content is embedded in the core game loop so that players must engage with the content to progress. DragonBox teaches algebra by making equation-solving the fundamental game mechanic.
Endogenous content is integral to gameplay (solving physics problems to build bridges); exogenous content is bolted on (answer a trivia question to earn a power-up)

learning_objectives: Design educational simulations that promote systems thinking
Determine appropriate fidelity levels for different learning objectives
Integrate debriefing as a critical learning component of simulation
readings: Aldrich, C. - Learning Online with Games, Simulations, and Virtual Worlds Ch. 4-6
Fanning, R. & Gaba, D. - The Role of Debriefing in Simulation-Based Learning

lecture_notes.introduction: Simulations allow learners to experiment with complex systems, make mistakes safely, and develop mental models through direct manipulation. From flight simulators to medical training to climate models, simulation is one of the most powerful learning technologies available.
key_concepts: Types of simulations: operational, conceptual, branching scenario
Fidelity spectrum: low-fidelity (paper-based) to high-fidelity (VR)
Scaffolding in simulations: worked examples, guided exploration, free exploration
Debriefing: the most critical part of simulation-based learning
Systems thinking: understanding feedback loops, emergent behavior, unintended consequences
lecture_notes.demonstration: Explore a systems dynamics simulation (e.g., World Climate or Fishbanks): run three scenarios, observe emergent behavior, and debrief using structured reflection
setup_requirements: Access to a systems dynamics simulation

review.value: Research consistently shows that learning from simulation occurs primarily during debriefing, not during play. Without structured reflection, students may develop incorrect mental models from their experience.
Higher fidelity increases realism but also increases cost and cognitive load. Novice learners often learn more from simplified, low-fidelity simulations that highlight key relationships.

learning_objectives: Design branching scenario structures for decision-making practice
Write realistic scenario narratives with consequential choices
Implement branching scenarios using authoring tools
readings: Shank, P. - Practice and Feedback for Deeper Learning Ch. 3
Allen, M. - Designing Successful e-Learning Ch. 7

lecture_notes.introduction: Branching scenarios present learners with realistic situations requiring decisions. Each choice leads to realistic consequences, allowing learners to experience cause-and-effect without real-world risk. They are particularly effective for soft skills, ethical dilemmas, and clinical reasoning.
key_concepts: Scenario structure: context, challenge, choice points, consequences, reflection
Decision tree vs. bottleneck design patterns
Writing authentic characters and situations
Feedback design: intrinsic (natural consequences) vs. extrinsic (coach feedback)
Tools: Twine, Articulate Storyline branching, H5P branching scenario
lecture_notes.demonstration: Build a branching scenario in Twine: create a 3-decision-point scenario for a workplace conflict resolution training, with at least 4 distinct endings
setup_requirements: Scenario design template with decision tree diagram

review.value: In a branching scenario, the most powerful feedback is seeing the natural consequences of your decision play out, rather than being told 'that was wrong' by an external narrator
All branches eventually converge to key decision points, keeping the scenario manageable while still providing meaningful choices and consequences

learning_objectives: Apply evidence-based gamification design patterns to educational contexts
Evaluate the risks and benefits of points, badges, and leaderboards
Design gamification that supports intrinsic motivation
readings: Deterding, S. et al. - From Game Design Elements to Gamefulness: Defining Gamification
Toda, A. et al. - A Taxonomy of Game Elements for Gamification in Educational Contexts

lecture_notes.introduction: Gamification is everywhere in education, but most implementations rely on the simplest elements (PBL: points, badges, leaderboards) without understanding the psychological mechanisms at work. Done poorly, gamification can undermine the intrinsic motivation it seeks to enhance.
key_concepts: PBL critique: when points and leaderboards harm motivation
Meaningful gamification: narrative, autonomy, mastery, social connection
Progression systems: experience points, skill trees, unlockable content
Social gamification: cooperation, competition, and their effects
Overjustification effect: extrinsic rewards undermining intrinsic interest
lecture_notes.demonstration: Gamification redesign: take a gamified LMS course that uses only PBL and redesign it with meaningful gamification elements that support self-determination theory
setup_requirements: Sample gamified course design for analysis

review.value: Nicholson's framework: use game design thinking (narrative, challenge, autonomy, social) rather than just game elements (points, badges) to create engagement grounded in intrinsic motivation
When students who were intrinsically motivated receive extrinsic rewards, their intrinsic motivation decreases. Particularly problematic with expected, tangible, task-contingent rewards.

learning_objectives: Apply evaluation frameworks specific to game-based learning
Design studies to assess the learning effectiveness of educational games
Critique published research on game-based learning
readings: Mayer, R. - Computer Games for Learning Ch. 10
Ke, F. - A Qualitative Meta-Analysis of Computer Games as Learning Tools

lecture_notes.introduction: How do we know if an educational game actually teaches? Evaluating games is more complex than evaluating traditional instruction because engagement and learning are intertwined but not identical. A game that is fun but does not teach has failed; a game that teaches but is not fun will not be played.
key_concepts: Four-dimensional evaluation: motivation, learning, transfer, efficiency
Value-added research design: game vs. equivalent non-game instruction
Engagement metrics vs. learning metrics: they are not the same
Playtesting: iterative user testing during development
Common methodological pitfalls in game-based learning research
lecture_notes.demonstration: Research critique exercise: evaluate a published study on game-based learning, identifying strengths, methodological weaknesses, and implications for design
setup_requirements: Published GBL research article for critique

review.value: The critical research question is not 'do students learn from games?' but 'do students learn MORE from games than from well-designed alternative instruction with equivalent content and time?'
High engagement does not guarantee high learning; students may enjoy a game but learn surface-level content. Evaluation must measure both independently.

quizzes.topic: Game-Based Learning Theory and Simulation Design

questions.question: An educational app awards 10 points for each completed reading and displays a class leaderboard. Using the concepts of gamification, game-based learning, and self-determination theory, evaluate this design.
questions.answer: This is gamification (adding game elements to a non-game task), not game-based learning. The design uses only extrinsic motivators (points and social comparison via leaderboard) without connecting them to intrinsic learning value. SDT concerns: the leaderboard may undermine autonomy (pressure to compete) and relatedness (students at the bottom feel excluded). Points for reading quantity may promote surface skimming rather than deep engagement. Improvement: replace points-per-reading with mastery-based progression tied to comprehension checks; add collaborative challenges instead of competitive leaderboards.

rubric.key: Correctly classifies as gamification, not GBL
Applies SDT to identify motivational risks
Proposes specific improvements grounded in theory

questions.question: Why is debriefing considered the most critical component of simulation-based learning? Describe the key elements of an effective debriefing session.
questions.answer: Debriefing is critical because experience alone does not guarantee learning. Without structured reflection, learners may form incorrect mental models, attribute outcomes to the wrong causes, or fail to transfer insights to new situations. Key elements: (1) Reaction phase — emotional processing of the experience. (2) Analysis phase — examining what happened, why, and what decisions were made. (3) Summary phase — extracting generalizable principles and identifying how insights apply to real-world practice. The facilitator guides reflection using open-ended questions rather than telling learners what they should have learned.

rubric.key: Explains why experience alone is insufficient for learning
Describes the phases or elements of effective debriefing
Emphasizes facilitated reflection over didactic telling

questions.question: Explain the difference between intrinsic feedback and extrinsic feedback in branching scenarios. Which type is generally more effective for learning and why?
questions.answer: Intrinsic feedback shows the natural consequences of the learner's decision within the scenario narrative (e.g., the patient's condition worsens after an incorrect medication choice). Extrinsic feedback is a message from outside the narrative (e.g., a pop-up saying 'That was incorrect. The right answer was B.'). Intrinsic feedback is generally more effective because it preserves immersion, makes consequences memorable and emotionally impactful, and helps learners build causal mental models. They experience why a decision was wrong, not just that it was wrong.

rubric.key: Accurate definitions of both feedback types
Correctly identifies intrinsic as generally more effective
Explains the cognitive/emotional mechanisms for effectiveness

questions.question: A researcher reports that students who played an educational history game scored 15% higher on a post-test than students who received no instruction. Why is this finding insufficient to claim the game is effective? What comparison group is needed?
questions.answer: Comparing game instruction to NO instruction only shows that some instruction is better than none — which is trivially true and tells us nothing about the value of the game format specifically. The appropriate comparison is a value-added design: compare the game group to students who received well-designed non-game instruction covering the same content in the same amount of time. This isolates the effect of the game design from the effect of simply receiving instruction on the topic.

rubric.key: Identifies the no-instruction comparison as insufficient
Explains what the comparison actually demonstrates
Describes the value-added design with matched comparison group

major_assignments.title: Educational Game or Simulation Design Document and Prototype
major_assignments.description: Design an educational game or simulation for a specific learning context. Produce a comprehensive design document including learning objectives, target audience analysis, LM-GM mapping, game flow diagram, feedback system design, and a playable prototype (can be paper-based, digital branching scenario, or digital game). Conduct a playtesting session with at least 3 users and report findings.
objectives: Apply game-based learning theory to an original game or simulation design
Create LM-GM alignment ensuring gameplay produces learning
Develop a playable prototype suitable for formative testing
Conduct and report playtesting with real users
deliverables: Game flow or branching scenario diagram
Playtesting report with user feedback and revision plan

criteria: Clear learning objectives aligned to game mechanics
Design decisions justified with GBL research
Engaging game mechanics with appropriate challenge progression
Clear rules, goals, and player agency
Demonstrates core game loop and learning mechanics
Structured playtesting with at least 3 users
Clear documentation of observations and feedback
Strong LM-GM alignment with intrinsic integration

quizzes.description: Bi-weekly quizzes on game-based learning theory and design principles

lab_assignments.description: Weekly design exercises: game analysis, scenario writing, prototype development

major_assignments.description: Educational game or simulation design and prototype project

midterm_examination.description: Covers GBL theory, LM-GM mapping, simulation design, and gamification

final_project.description: Revised game/simulation with extended playtesting and evaluation report

course_policies.attendance: Attendance is required for all playtesting and design review sessions. More than 2 unexcused absences will reduce the final grade.
course_policies.late_submissions: Late work loses 5% per day for up to 5 days. Playtesting deadlines are firm due to peer participation requirements.
course_policies.collaboration: Playtesting requires peer participation. Game design documents and prototypes must be individually created unless group projects are approved in advance.
course_policies.academic_integrity: All game designs must be original. Use of existing game assets requires proper licensing and attribution. Violations result in a zero and referral.
course_policies.technology_requirements: Computer with internet access. Free accounts for Twine and/or H5P. Recommended: Articulate Storyline trial or Unity for digital prototypes.
